Enzyme hybrid nanoflowers and enzyme@metal–organic frameworks composites: fascinating hybrid nanobiocatalysts

Zichen Wanga State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Laboratory of Industrial Fermentation Microbiology, Ministry of Education, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin Economic and Technological Development Area (TEDA), Tianjin, China
,
Ruirui Wanga State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Laboratory of Industrial Fermentation Microbiology, Ministry of Education, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin Economic and Technological Development Area (TEDA), Tianjin, China
,
Zixin Genga State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Laboratory of Industrial Fermentation Microbiology, Ministry of Education, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin Economic and Technological Development Area (TEDA), Tianjin, China
,

Abstract

Hybrid nanomaterials have recently emerged as a new interface of nanobiocatalysis, serving as a host platform for enzyme immobilization. Enzyme immobilization in inorganic crystal nanoflowers and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) has sparked the bulk of scientific interest due to their superior performances. Many breakthroughs have been achieved recently in the preparation of various types of enzyme@MOF and enzyme-hybrid nanoflower composites. However, it is unfortunate that there are few reviews in the literature related to enzyme@MOF and enzyme-hybrid nanoflower composites and their improved synthesis strategies and their applications in biotechnology. In this review, innovative synthetic strategies for enzyme@MOF composites and enzyme-hybrid nanoflower composites are discussed. Enzyme@MOF composites and enzyme-hybrid nanoflower composites are reviewed in terms of biotechnological applications and potential research directions. We are convinced that a fundamental study and application of enzyme@MOF composites and enzyme-hybrid nanoflower composites will be understood by the reader as a result of this work. The summary of different synthetic strategies for enzyme@MOF composites and enzyme-hybrid nanoflower composites and the improvement of their synthetic strategies will also benefit the readers and provide ideas and thoughts in the future research process.

Keywords:

  • Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs)
  • enzyme hybrid nanoflowers
  • enzyme immobilization
  • new synthesizing strategies

Introduction

As the biocatalyst, enzyme catalysis is a nonpolluting, sustainable, and efficient catalyst that accelerates reactions by reducing the activation energy of substances [Citation1]. Due to their diversity and specificity, enzymes have recently received a lot of attention and are used in various fields, such as medicine, biosensing, and industrial production. However, practical applications regularly involve higher temperatures, organic solvents, and extreme pH values, leading to instability or even inactivation of the enzyme. Furthermore, recovering the free enzyme from the reaction solution is relatively difficult, which directly increases the cost and leads to a waste of resources [Citation2]. Over the past two decades, various materials including mesoporous silica, chitosan, graphene as well as sodium alginate have been used as carriers to immobilize enzymes through encapsulation, adsorption, covalent binding, and cross-linking. Moreover, some advances have been made in terms of recovery efficiency and meeting actual reaction conditions [Citation3–7]. However, immobilized enzymes obtained using conventional carriers still suffer from low immobilization efficiency, enzyme leakage, easy inactivation, and high mass transfer resistance, due to the limitations of carrier features, including small specific surface area, irregular pore size distribution, poor biocompatibility, and complex synthesis process. Therefore, the design of favorable carriers with appropriate methods remains a challenge for enzyme immobilization [Citation8,Citation9]. In the past few years, hybrid nanomaterials represent a new interface for nanobiotechnology catalysis and serve as a host platform for immobilized enzymes. In particular, immobilized enzymes derived from inorganic nanoflowers and MOFs have attracted the interest of academics thanks to their minimal synthesis and extreme efficiency [Citation8–11].

MOFs are a family of organic-inorganic hybrid nanocrystalline porous materials consisting of metal ions or clusters connected to organic ligands via coordination bonds [Citation12–14]. MOFs have recently been favored in the fields of gas storage, separation, pharmaceuticals, and biocatalysis by researchers due to their specific characteristics, such as modifiable porosity, elevated concrete surface area, and diverse structures as well as abundant interaction sites. Recently, MOFs have exhibited a positive performance as carriers for immobilized enzymes. Furthermore, the resultant enzyme@MOFs composites exhibited excellent catalytic activity and protein loading [Citation9].

For example, Shen et al. generated significant biocatalytic capacity by encapsulating the free enzyme in MOFs with iron and cobalt bimetals as metal sources, resulting in an almost three-fold increase in immobilized enzyme activity compared to a simple biocatalyst mixture in solution [Citation15]. Shen et al. synthesized multifunctional multi-enzyme cascade nanoplatforms with excellent biosensing activity based on MOFs through a microwave-assisted co-assembly strategy. The cascade nanoplatform combines the advantages of both the artificial nanoplatform and the natural enzyme and possesses a good sarcosine/acetylcholine sensing capability [Citation16]. Feng et al. used polystyrene (PS) nanospheres as a template to synthesize novel amino-functionalized ZIF-8 with a three-dimensional highly ordered macroporous structure, and the material was magnetic due to the intervention of magnetic nanoparticles during the synthesis process [Citation10]. Subsequently, CAT monomers were immobilized on the surface and in the pores of the macroporous magnetic ZIF-8 through a precipitated cross-linked manner and the resulting immobilized enzyme showed high immobilization efficiency and excellent recoverability.

As another popular hybrid nanomaterial in the field of immobilized enzymes, enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers are characterized by: a simple preparation process, the high enzymatic activity of the prepared hybrid nanoflower immobilized enzymes, and good stability [Citation8]. Unlike the formation process of conventional immobilized enzymes, enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers are formed by the self-assembly of enzymes and phosphate metal salts, and the synthesis process is divided into three steps: nucleation, anisotropic growth, and formation of flower shape [Citation17]. So far, a variety of enzymes, such as lipase, catalase(CAT), horseradish peroxidase(HRP), trypsin, urease, glucose oxidase(GOx), papain, laccase(Lac), α-lactase, and soybean peroxidase have been used to synthesize enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers. For most enzymes, enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflower technology can not only substantially increase their enzymatic activities but also enhance their operational stability, storage stability, and reusability.

Liu et al. immobilized thermophilic lipase in Cu3(PO4)2-based inorganic hybrid nanoflowers by biomimetic mineralization. In contrast to the free liapse, the liapse-HNF showed excellent stability over heat, pH, and metal ions. In addition, the hybrid nanoflower displayed superior reusability [Citation18]. Wen et al. synthesized a bimetallic-based inorganic-carbonic anhydrase (CA) hybrid nanoflower. The synthesized bimetallic (Zn and Cu) hybrid nanoflower (CANF) was subsequently embedded in a poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA)-chitosan (CS) hydrogel network to obtain a PVA/CS@CANF hydrogel membrane. The PVA/CS @CANF exhibited higher enzymatic properties than free CA and CANF and showed a modest improvement in reusability and CO2 capture [Citation19].

Nevertheless, with continuous research, MOFs present some adverse effects, such as: small pore size, acid instability, hydrophobicity, and precursor properties, which inhibit the activity and stability of MOFs@enzyme complexes [Citation20–23]. Similarly, researchers also found that hybrid nanoflowers still have problems, such as low mechanical strength due to the laminar petal structure of nanoflowers, ease of being broken and lost during the recovery process, resulting in the loss of enzyme activity, and challenging to be separated in the reaction solution and easily soluble in acidic solution [Citation19,Citation24]. Therefore, to overcome these drawbacks, it is necessary to develop new and improved synthesis strategies.

Although several reviews regarding the immobilization of enzymes in hybrid nanoflowers or MOFs have appeared during the last few years [Citation8,Citation9,Citation25,Citation26], the rapid development of novel and improved approaches to the enzymes@MOF composites and hybrid nanoflowers needs to be updated and reviewed in a timely manner. In this review, we summarize some of the latest developments in enzymes@MOF composites as well as hybrid nanoflower immobilized enzymes, with a particular focus on new and improved strategies and possible biotechnological applications. Meanwhile, the prospective future of these novel immobilized enzyme strategies are summarily explored.

General strategies for synthesizing enzyme@MOF composites

So far, the methods of immobilizing enzymes using MOFs as carriers mainly include physical adsorption, covalent binding, cage inclusion, and co-precipitation. These methods each have their own advantages and disadvantages. Therefore, different immobilization methods need to be selected according to the characteristics of MOFs themselves and the properties of the enzymes to be immobilized.

Physical adsorption

Physisorption is a convenient and economy-friendly way to immobilize enzymes by MOFs, relying on weak forces between the enzyme and the MOFs (e.g., hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, ionic binding, hydrophobic interactions, van der Waals forces, etc.) to bind the enzyme to the surface of the MOFs (). This process does not involve the chemical modification of the enzyme [Citation9,Citation27]. Derived from the dynamic equilibrium nature of physisorption, the weak forces on which physisorption can act as traction on the enzyme molecule, reducing activity loss [Citation27]. The firmness of physisorption between enzymes and MOFs depends mainly on the interface features between MOFs and enzymes, such as polarizability, charge, magnetic induction strength, and dipole moment [Citation28]. Physisorption, as a simple and green way to immobilize enzymes, is characterized by low cost, low contamination, and no need to introduce additional reagents. Even though active enzyme molecules can be fixed on the surface of MOFs through physical adsorption, there are still many enzymes that exhibit poor stability due to only weak interactions with MOFs. The emergence of covalent binding seems to solve these problems [Citation28].

Figure 1. Enzyme immobilization on MOFs by surface immobilization.

Covalent binding

Generally speaking, MOFs present a wide range of multifunctional moieties (e.g., free amide, carboxyl, hydroxyl, epoxy, and ethoxy moieties) on their surface, which enable the tight binding of enzyme molecules to MOFs by forming covalent bonds with the corresponding reactive moieties on the surface of the enzyme molecules [Citation29]. Covalent binding is considered to be a more stable method of immobilization because of the creation of novel chemical bonds with MOFs and enzymes (). The covalent bond formation reduces the flexibility of the conformational phase of the enzyme protein, thus preventing the unfolding and denaturation of the protein [Citation9,Citation28]. For example, the free amino group on the surface of the enzyme or MOF is bound to their carboxyl groups, respectively, forming a peptide bond. Recently it has been reported that substances, such as 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC), sulfo-N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide (Sulfo-NHS), dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) and glutaraldehyde, are added and introduced as functional additives during immobilization to promote the activation of the reactive groups. At the same time, immobilized enzymes by covalent linkage need to fulfill relevant conditions, including the choice of promising material properties (high inner surface as well as the maximum concentration of reactive groups), the characteristics of the mobile group (minimal hindrance to reaction with enzymes, high stability) and appropriate anchoring parameters (favoring both reactivities of the groups and flexibility of the enzyme molecule) [Citation30]. However, the formation of covalent bonding between MOFs and enzyme proteins also affects the conformation of the enzyme, resulting in a reduction in enzymatic activity. Therefore, how to avoid contact between the cross-linking agent and the amino acid residues around the active site of the enzyme protein, and how to protect the amino acid residues of the enzyme, becomes important factor for the further development of the covalent cross-linking method.

Figure 2. Enzyme immobilization on MOFs by covalent linkage.

Cage inclusion

Due to their loose and porous structure, the enzyme molecules can penetrate into the pores and the mesoporous MOFs act as “cages”to retain the enzymes [Citation31] (). The immobilized enzymes by this synthetic method have a high recovery of enzyme activity due to high pore size and a vast passage space within the MOF matrix. At the same time, the stability of the enzyme in severe conditions, as well as in unusual conditions, can be significantly enhanced due to the protective effect of MOFs compared to surface immobilization [Citation9]. Not only that, this immobilization method confines the enzyme in a three-dimensional microenvironment that can minimize protein folding. However, the pore size of current MOFs is generally microporous, so the way to make the enzyme molecules diffusely retained in the pore size of MOFs appears to be very difficult for larger enzyme molecules and cannot achieve a satisfactory immobilization effect [Citation10,Citation32]. Therefore, it is an urgent problem to propose new immobilization methods to confine the enzyme within MOFs.

Figure 3. Enzyme immobilization into the pores of MOF by diffusion.

Co-precipitation

Recently, a one-pot imbedding technique has been able to immobilize enzymes with sizes significantly larger than the aperture of the carriers in MOFs with narrow pores [Citation29]. In this immobilization of enzymes using this embedding strategy, nucleation, crystallization, and immobilization of the enzyme by the MOF are carried out simultaneously [Citation33], which is otherwise called as the “in situ embedding” tactics and often involves co-precipitation and biomineralization processes [Citation34] (). During immobilization, some functional additives(e.g., polyvinylpyrrolidone, PVP) are added to the reaction system to facilitate the bioencapsulation process of enzymes [Citation29,Citation35]. Compared to other immobilization strategies, in situ embedding is a more concise immobilization approach that simplifies the operation by combining two separate steps (i.e., synthesis of MOFs and immobilization of the enzyme) into one. Therefore, one-pot embedding strategies seem more suitable for immobilizing enzymes within MOFs than adsorption and covalent cross-linking. However, it has been reported that the prepared microenvironment or the precursor of the synthesized material may affect the structural changes of the enzyme during immobilization, reducing or even inactivating the enzyme.

Figure 4. Enzyme encapsulation into MOFs by co-precipitation.

Improvement strategies for synthesizing enzyme@MOF composites

Although enzyme@MOF composites have shown a certain superiority in different fields, MOFs likewise present some negative effects, such as small pore size, acid instability, hydrophobicity, and precursor properties, which inhibit the activity and stability of enzyme@MOFs composites [Citation10,Citation36]. To address these problems, new improved strategies in recent years, such as aperture engineering, surface functionalization, ligand hybrid functionalization, and magnetic hybrid, have recently been proposed to overcome the negative effects of MOFs for improving the activity and stability of enzyme@MOFs complexes [Citation37].

Aperture engineering

Unlike soluble free enzymes, immobilized enzymes require a substrate or product to enter or separate from the enzyme molecule through the carrier during the catalytic reaction, so porous carriers are more suitable for enzyme immobilization. Usually, porous materials mainly consist of: micropores (<2 nm), mesopores (2–50 nm), and macropores (>50 nm), while the latter two are relatively more desirable for enzyme immobilization [Citation10]. However, the majority of MOFs discussed till now generally have their own pore size of less than 2 nm, which makes it difficult for most enzyme molecules to enter the pores of MOFs using adsorption or diffusion, and the small pore size also generates a large mass transfer resistance and reduces the catalytic efficiency of immobilized enzymes [Citation38]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to adjust the pore size of MOFs to microporous or even macroporous levels to better achieve immobilized enzymes.

Recently, some efforts have been made in the synthesis of MOFs with hierarchical micropores and mesopores (HMOFs) [Citation39]. In general, the synthesis strategies of HMOFs mainly contains: extension of ligand, hybrid ligands, defect formation strategies, and template- (hard and soft) assisted synthesis [Citation38]. Among them, defect-forming strategies and template-assisted synthesis have been more maturely applied to convert MOFs to HMOFs. The resulting immobilized enzymes show significant improvements in mass transfer and catalytic efficiency. For instance, Hu et al. adopted a microfluidic system to adjust the flow rates of the precursor zinc ions and 2-methylimidazole, leading to a lack of coordination and the appearance of mesoporous structures [Citation40] (). The pore width increased from 3 to 20 nm when the flow rate was raised by a factor of 5 and 20. After embedding the enzyme inside the mesopores, the GOx-MOFs composites exhibited 98% of native GOx activity, which was higher than the GOx-MOFs composites synthesized in bulk solution. Cheng et al. prepared the hydrogel initially and subsequently encapsulated it within ZIF-8 as a template, removing the hydrogel under high-temperature conditions to form HZIF-8 [Citation41]. The average pore size and pore volume of the prepared HZIF-8 were 16.2 nm and 0.57 mL/g, respectively. Meanwhile, GOx and HRP were adsorbed inside the mesopores of HZIF-8. Compared with the conventional microporous GOx-HRP@ZIF-8, the Km value of GOx-HRP@HZIF-8 decreased to 30% due to the formation of a mesoporous structure. Moreover, the Kcat/Km of GOx-HRP@HZIF-8 was calculated as 85.68 mM/s, which was 6.7 times and 1.7 times higher than free enzymes and GOx-HRP@ZIF-8, respectively. In addition, Feng et al. used ordered polystyrene microspheres(3DPS) as templates to grow ZIF-8 crystals between the gaps of 3DPS and then removed the templates to form macroporous ZIF-8 with ordered pore structures [Citation10]. The average pore size of the macroporous ZIF-8 was about 69 nm. After the enzyme was immobilized on macroporous ZIF-8 by the co-precipitation cross-linking method, the recovery of enzyme activity and immobilization efficiency were increased by 50%, and the Km value was decreased by 10%, respectively. Although aperture engineering can improve the pore size of MOFs and increase the activity of enzyme@MOFs complexes, its complex preparation process limits its wide use.

Figure 5. Schematics of microfluidic laminar flow synthesis and bulk solution synthesis of enzyme@MOF composites. (a) Synthesis of enzyme-MOF composites in a microfluidic laminar flow. Zinc nitrate, 2-MeIM, and enzymes were introduced into the three inlets; enzyme-MOF composites were formed in the microchannel via diffusion-based gradient mixing. (b) Synthesis of enzyme-MOF composites in bulk solution. The reactants were added into the beaker and mixed through stirring.

Surface functionalization

In order to increase the scope of the application of MOF materials in the field of immobilized enzymes, the functionalization of MOFs by modifying the surface of MOFs with chemical groups for enzyme immobilization has attracted attention [Citation42]. Compared to the use of unmodified MOFs, functionalized MOFs have more covalent immobilization sites for binding to the enzyme, thus not only conferring higher protein loading to the MOF material but also bringing higher stability to the immobilized enzyme as well. A series of studies revealed that ZIFs lost their stability and showed decomposition under acidic conditions [Citation43]. Jia et al. found that under acidic conditions, ZIFs started to become unstable and high concentrations of Zn2+ were released into the supernatant, indicating that the ZIF-8 particles were solubilized [Citation44]. Moreover, this defect of ZIF-8 further affected the activity and stability of enzyme@ZIFs. To solve these problems, some researchers have proposed to functionalize the surface of MOFs by depositing or wrapping related materials on the surface of MOFs to improve their performance as a carrier for immobilized enzymes.

For example, to improve the acid resistance of ZIFs, mesoporous silica layers were formed on the surface of CAT/ZIFs composites by controlled self-assembly and condensation of silanes. The resulting mSiO2@CAT/ZIF composite exhibited better thermal stability, storage, and reusability compared to conventional CAT/ZIF composites [Citation36] (). The immobilized enzyme was shown to be cruciform before and after mSiO2 coating by TEM images, indicating that the formation of the silica shell did not affect the shape of the immobilized enzyme. In addition, PXRD maps showed that the crystal structure of mSiO2@CAT/ZIFs was consistent with that of CAT/ZIFs, further demonstrating that the presence of a silica layer did not significantly change the crystal structure. Especially, the mSiO2@CAT/ZIFs had a strong acid resistance compared to the conventional CAT/ZIFs composites. The same group then proposed a method to prepare CAT/ZIF-8 composites with protective nanocoatings by controlling the self-assembly process of silanes and ligand complexes (tannic acid (TA) and Fe3+), CAT/ZIF-8 with protective nanocoatings also exhibited excellent acid resistance.

Figure 6. The synthesis process of CAT/ZIFs and mSiO2@CAT/ZIFs. Reproduced from ref. [Citation33] with permission from Elsevier, Copyright 2019.

Due to their excellent water stability and acid resistance, Ravastaine backbone (MILs) materials promise to be excellent carriers for enzyme immobilization. Unfortunately, immobilization efficiency is very low because of the tiny pore size and scarce amino groups of MIL, Wang et al. prepared amino-functionalized MIL-125-NH2 by using (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES) [Citation45]. The amino-functionalized MIL-125-NH2 had more amino groups on its surface compared to MIL-125-NH2, and the immobilized enzyme on amino-functionalized MIL-125-NH2 exhibited four-fold higher enzyme activity recovery than the unaminated MIL-125-NH2. Furthermore, the immobilized enzyme on amino-functionalized MIL-125-NH2 showed good pH stability, stability to denaturant, and thermal stability.

Although the immobilization of enzymes using functionally modified MOFs can improve some of the properties of immobilized enzymes, the application of this method is limited to a certain extent due to the complexity of the modification process and the difficulty of control [Citation42]. Therefore, developing hybrid materials based on MOF materials will potentially provide a new and efficient immobilization strategy for immobilizing enzymes.

Hybrid functionalization

In a one-pot imbedding technique, enzyme activity usually decreases during immobilization due to conformational changes in the prepared microenvironment or the precursor of the synthesized material [Citation34]. For example, imidazole moiety is prone to protonation in solution, creating a strongly basic environment that poses a significant threat to enzyme activity. Chen et al. revealed that cytochrome C (Cyt C) and GOx were largely inactive after simultaneous exposure to high concentrations of imidazole for 10 min. The iron porphyrin ring of Cyt C underwent conformational changes in fluorescence spectra compared to its initial state [Citation46]. Moreover, it has also been reported that in most cases, for soluble Cyt C, the exogenous ligand binds to ferroportin and replaces the initial methionine ligand, causing a series of changes in redox potential and spectral properties. In addition, the interfacial interactions between MOFs and enzymes, such as electrostatic interactions, hydrophilic/hydrophobic interactions, and reorientation of enzymes, also influence the immobilization effects to different degrees. Therefore, it is crucial to take some measures to maintain the structural changes of the enzyme over the course of immobilization [Citation47].

The synthesis of novel MOFs for enzyme immobilization by hybridizing MOF conventional ligands using competitive ligands has received increasing attention and reports. The competitive ligands used are mostly bioaffinity ligands, such as amino acids and short peptide molecules, etc. The addition of these bioaffinity ligands can improve the microenvironment and the interaction between the enzyme and the hybridized MOFs, and slow down the damage of the conventional organic ligands of MOFs to the enzyme molecules during immobilization, achieving a simple and rapid one-step immobilization. Ouyang et al. reported a novel amino acid as a competitive ligand for heterogeneous immobilization, which improved the carrier crystal structure of the MOF and the microenvironment in which the enzyme molecule resides and obtained a high catalytic activity [Citation48] (). Their results showed that amino acids can facilitate the bionic mineralization process and can achieve rapid encapsulation of enzyme proteins, resulting in MOFs with ultra-high enzyme protein sequestration efficiency.

Figure 7. Schematic representation of the amino-acid-boosted one-pot embedding (AAOPE) strategy.

More recently, the results of a new study have shown that the solubility of ZIFs in an acidic solution environment is negatively correlated with their own hydrophilicity and the organic linkers that constitute their crystal structure. From this perspective, Gao et al. proposed a new approach to improve the chemical stability of ZIFs by partially replacing the imidazole linker of perfect Zn N tetrahedra with a functional group having a higher pK a value. PGA@ZIFs were prepared by introducing carboxyl-rich poly-L-glutamic acid in the synthesis of ZIFs. The characteristic peaks of pure ZIFs were completely disappeared in the SAXS graphs after immersing ZIFs and PGA@ZIFs in acetate buffer (50 mM, pH 5) for 20 min under acidic conditions. In contrast, the PGA@ZIFs were able to maintain their diffraction and porosity after nearly 3 h of treatment, which indicated that the acid tolerance of PGA@ZIFs was significantly higher than that of pure ZIFs [Citation43].

Magnetic hybrid

In the process of immobilization of enzymes, the homogeneous morphology and good dispersion characteristics of MOF are the reasons why it stands out among the many carriers used for the immobilization of enzymes. However, it is difficult to recover and recycle enzyme@MOF composites by centrifugation, precipitation, and filtration due to their: high dispersion, low density, and nano-size, which directly leads to poor reusability and activity recovery in practical applications, resulting in cost and resource wastage [Citation10].

As a unique carrier to immobilize enzymes, magnetic nanoparticles have the advantages, such as good biocompatibility, tiny particle size, high specific surface area, super paramagnetic, high enzyme loading capacity, and stable presence in solution. Therefore, they can be compounded with a variety of materials, and the unique physical or surface properties of these composites can be used to improve the immobilization efficiency of enzymes and solve the defective problems that exist in the field of immobilized enzymes with single materials [Citation49].

Wang et al. developed a surfactant-free, low-cost, environmentally friendly, non-agglomerating Fe3O4@MIL-100(Fe) core/shell composite microspheres with appropriate pore size (7.07 nm), large specific surface area (137.27 m2/g) and high magnetization strength (49.67 emu/g) [Citation50]. The lipase of Pseudostelium was immobilized on magnetic core/shell composite microspheres. After hydrolysis of olive oil at 65 °C for 6 h, the immobilized enzyme could maintain approximately 65% of its initial activity. Furthermore, after 10 consecutive cycles, as much as 60% of the initial activity of the immobilized enzyme was still maintained. Ladole et al. synthesized citrate-modified Fe3O4 (CA-Fe3O4) particles with CA-Fe3O4 as the core [Citation51] and laccase was embedded inside the MOFs while forming MOFs crystals on their surface [Citation52]. The resulting magnetic MOFs (MMOFs) possess peroxidase activity, which can act synergistically with laccase to produce a high degree of degradation of industrial dyes in a relatively short time. Furthermore, the immobilized enzyme exhibited good reusability due to the presence of magnetic nanoparticles and maintained up to 89% residual activity after the 10th cycle, resulting in a sustained degradation efficiency of 95% for industrial dyes.

General strategies for synthesizing enzyme–inorganic hybrid nanoflowers

Self-assembly is common in nature, for example, cells of living organisms are formed by self-assembly of various biological molecules; and the use of various molecular self-assembly is also a very important method for constructing nanomaterials. The so-called bottom-up approach of molecular self-assembly is now widely used to prepare nanomaterials with catalytic functions.

In 2012, Ge et al. designed a novel approach to immobilize enzymes based on the principle of molecular self-assembly, i.e., enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflower technology [Citation17]. The method is characterized by: a simple preparation process, the high enzymatic activity of the prepared hybrid nanoflower immobilized enzyme, and good stability. Unlike the formation process of traditional immobilized enzymes, enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers are formed by the self-assembly of enzyme and phosphate metal salts, and the synthesis process is divided into three steps: nucleation, anisotropic growth, and formation of flower shape [Citation8] ().

Figure 8. The formation of hybrid nanoflowers is illustrated with three steps: (1) nucleation and formation stage of primary crystals; (2) growth stage of crystals; and (3) formation stage of nanoflowers.

Until now, a variety of biomolecules including various enzymes, such as lipase [Citation53], horseradish peroxidase [Citation54], trypsin [Citation55], urease [Citation56], papain [Citation57], laccase [Citation58], α-lactase [Citation59], and soybean peroxidase [Citation20] have been used to synthesize enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers. In addition, various metal ions, such as silver ions [Citation53], calcium ions [Citation21], copper ions [Citation22], zinc ions [Citation23], manganese ions [Citation60], and cobalt ions [Citation61], were also used to synthesize enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers. In general, biomolecules (including proteins, nucleic acids, enzymes, and chlorogenic acid) contain metal binding sites that are capable of forming complexes with metal ions through coordination. Enzyme-hybrid nanoflowers can be prepared with metal ions with different valence states. In this review, we first summarized the relevant reports and applications of enzyme-hybrid nanoflowers based on the differences in metal ion valence states ().

Table 1. Enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers prepared from different metal ions.

Download CSVDisplay Table

Hybrid nanoflowers using monovalent metal ion

The cases of monovalent metal ions as inorganic ligands involved in synthesizing organic-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers are few compared to other valence states of metal ions, and it is generally customary to use monovalent silver ions as metal ion sources involved in the synthesis of nanoflowers. For example, Sharma and his team prepared hybrid nanoflowers of lipase derived from Burkholderia cepacia (BCL) using monovalent silver ions as an inorganic metal source and investigated their related enzymatic properties [Citation53]. The resultant lipase-inorganic nanoflowers showed 5-fold higher specific activity than the free lipase. However, different from the results of Sharma et al. the prepared lipase (from Aspergillus oryzae) hybrid nanoflowers by using silver ions by Li et al. exhibited low activity. It was explained that the enzyme species are different and do not have the same tolerance to silver ions [Citation62].

Hybrid nanoflowers using divalent metal ion

Divalent metal ions (including copper ions, manganese ions, calcium ions, zinc ions, ferrous ions, etc.) are the most frequently reported metal ion populations in synthesizing enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers. It is interesting to note that the prepared hybrid nanoflowers with the same enzyme and different divalent metal ions can exhibit different functional properties.

Copper(II) ions

For the first time, Ge and coworkers combined laccase with copper phosphate to produce catalase-Cu nanoflowers with six times the free enzyme activity, and the laccase-Cu nanoflowers exhibited better temperature tolerance [Citation17]. In addition to this enzyme-copper phosphate hybrid nanoflowers, including urease [Citation56], trypsin [Citation55], glucoamylase [Citation69], papain[Citation57], laccase [Citation92], glucose oxidase [Citation93], lactoperoxidase [Citation94] and L-arabinose isomerase [Citation68], were prepared, and exhibited much higher catalytic activity and stability than the free enzymes.

Generally, the excellent enzymatic activity performance exhibited by the enzyme-copper phosphate hybrid nanoflowers is mainly attributed to the following aspects. (1) The structural characteristics of the nanoflower itself cause it to have a very high specific surface area, allowing the enzyme molecules distributed on the petals to come into better contact with the substrate, thus reducing the mass transfer resistance to the reaction; (2) Cooperative effects of nanoscale encapsulated enzyme molecules; (3) The interaction of the enzyme with the metal ions causes certain changes in the conformation of the enzyme protein, thus increasing its enzymatic activity [Citation8,Citation17].

Calcium ions

As mentioned earlier, metal ions play a crucial role in the enzymatic activity exhibited by synthetic enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers. Recently, calcium ions have been used to prepare enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers [Citation95]. Ke et al. prepared BCL-Ca3(PO4)2 hybrid nanoflowers by combining Burkholderia lipase (BCL) with calcium phosphate. They found that the secondary structure of BCL was modified following the creation of BCL-Ca3(PO4)2 hybrid nanoflowers [Citation96]. Li et al. found that calcium ions have an activating effect on lipase compared to other metal ions and that calcium ions induced a change in lipase conformation during the synthesis of lipase-Ca3(PO4)2 hybrid nanoflowers [Citation62]. Based on the effect of calcium ions on the amide group of amylase, Maurya and coworkers discovered a method to synthesize alpha-amylase hybrid biomicroflavours within 6 h. The resultant amylase hybrid nanoflowers exhibited a 1.4-fold increase in catalytic activity and good reusability. Based on this same mechanism, hybrid CaHPO4-α-amylase nanoflowers were prepared and showed significantly improved enzymatic activity [Citation97]. Recently, Sun and coworkers prepared PAL@Ca3(PO4)2 hybrid nanoflowers (PAL@NF) using phenylalanine ammonia-lyase (PAL) and Ca2+ and used them to construct a new paper-based biosensor, which allowed the detection of phenylalanine in urine samples with a wide linear range and fast reaction time. The flower-like shape of PAL@NF was demonstrated by TEM images, and intense green fluorescence was observed in CLSM images of PAL@NF (PAL proteins were labeled by FITC), indicating the presence of PAL in PAL@NF. The characteristic peaks obtained by FTIR spectral analysis from 1060 cm–1 to 1030 cm–1 and from 610 cm–1 to 530 cm–1 were attributed to phosphate groups. Two intense typical characteristic peaks were observed between 1660 cm−1 to 1650 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 to 1500 cm−1, which are considered as the amide I and II bands of the protein, indicating the presence of PAL in PAL@NF and proving that the preparation of PAL@NF was successful [Citation11] ().

Figure 9. Paper-based biosensor based on phenylalnine ammonia lyase hybrid nanoflowers for urinary phenylalanine measurement. Reproduced from ref. [Citation11] with permission from Elsevier, Copyright 2020.

Zinc ions

The quick reaction of zinc ions with phosphate allows them to accelerate the formation of nanoflowers, reducing the preparation time and minimizing the damage to the enzyme [Citation23]. Zhang et al. prepared lipase-zinc phosphate hybrid nanoflowers, which were rapidly synthesized and exhibited a nearly 1.5-fold increase in enzymatic activity compared to free lipase [Citation98]. Besides, the adsorption capacity of the nanoflowers was also increased by electrostatic interactions between metal ions and the amino acid residues of the BSA. On the basis of this discovery, Zhang et al. synthesized red blood cell-like bovine serum albumin (BSA)/Zn3(PO4)2 hybrid nanoflowers [Citation23]. The prepared BSA/Zn3(PO4)2 hybrid nanoflowers exhibited efficient adsorption of copper ions. Patel et al. synthesized β-glucosidase (BGL)-Zn3(PO4)2 HNFs that increased enzyme activity nearly 2-fold compared to free BGL. More importantly, the hybrid nanoflowers prepared from Zn ions showed the highest enzymatic activity compared to other metal ions under the same conditions [Citation84].

Ferrous ions

Enzyme-iron phosphate hybrid nanoflowers have been favored by researchers in recent years due to their: low toxicity, safety, bio-friendliness and simplicity, and low cost of preparation. Ocsoy et al. produced the first Fe2+-HRP heterogeneous nanoflowers (HNF) in 2015. The resulting HNF displayed extremely beneficial storage stability and higher enzymatic activity than the free enzyme, which is attributed to the reduced mass transfer resistance due to the HNF structure and the activation of HRP by Fe2+ ions [Citation54]. Based on this mechanism, Guo et al. prepared glucose oxidase-iron phosphate hybrid nanoflowers (GOx-Fe3(PO4)2) for the rapid detection of glucose [Citation85]. Compared to free glucose oxidase, the hybrid nanoflower showed better enzymatic activity, stability, and reusability and successfully achieved good accuracy in the detection of glucose in human serum.

Manganese ions

Recently, Rai and coworkers have prepared L-arabinose isomerase-Mn3(PO4)2 hybrid nanoflowers to synthesize D-tagatose. The L-arabinose isomerase-NHF shows good repeatability and provided a potential for the commercial production of the rare sugar d-tagatose [Citation54]. In addition, based on good electrochemical activity, manganese phosphate was used to synthesize enzyme-manganese phosphate nanoflowers for electrochemical biosensors [Citation99]. Gao et al. have synthesized hemoglobin (Hb)-Mn3(PO4)2 hybrid nanoflowers (HMHNFs) to generate hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) electrochemical biosensors. The designed biosensor exhibits an extremely low detection limit and a large linear range. This excellent H2O2 sensing performance can be attributed to the unfolding of Hb and the high specific surface of HMHNF, it generates a large number of exposed electroactive centers for the reduction of H2O2 [Citation100].

Hybrid nanoflowers using trivalent metal ions

Compared to divalent metal ions, the involvement of trivalent metal ions in enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers appears to be somewhat sparse. The trivalent metal ions in nanoflower synthesis are generally concentrated in trivalent gold ions and trivalent aluminum ions [Citation53,Citation62]. Sharma et al. prepared hybrid nanoflowers of lipases derived from Borrelia burgdorferi (BCL) using trivalent gold ions as an inorganic metal source. The resultant hybrid nanoflower displayed good enzymatic activity and stability [Citation53] ().

Figure 10. Synthesis schematic of PVA/CS@CANF hydrogel.

Hybrid nanoflowers using mixed valence metal ions

It is well known that hybrid nanoflowers are formed by coordination between enzymes and metal ions [Citation8,Citation17]. Metal ions greatly influence the morphology of hybrid nanoflowers and enzyme activity. Recently, the emergence of some polymetallic inorganic hybrid nanoflowers has provided new ideas. Patel et al. developed a novel polymetallic-based inorganic-protein hybrid nanoflowers, synthesizing a polymetallic Cu3/Zn3 (PO4)2-laccase hybrid (Cu/Zn-Lac) with an encapsulation yield of 96.5% and a much higher enzyme activity than the prepared hybrid nanoflowers using a single metal ion [Citation91]. The polymetallic hybrid nanoflowers also showed good reusability. Recently, a bimetallic-carbonic anhydrase (CA) hybrid nanoflower based on copper and zinc ions was developed and subsequently combined with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)-chitosan (CS) hydrogels to obtain PVA/CS@CANF hydrogels. The PVA/CS@CANF hydrogels exhibited good enzymatic properties and reusability [Citation19] ().

Figure 11. General scheme for the preparation of combined nanoflowers.

Improvement strategies for synthesizing enzyme–inorganic hybrid nanoflowers

Since the discovery of enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflower immobilized enzymes in 2012, enzyme hybrid nanoflower has become a research hotspot. Although most reports revealed that enzyme hybrid nanoflowers exhibited excellent activity and stability, hybrid nanoflowers still have some disadvantages: (1) hybrid nanoflowers are easily broken and lost during the recovery process due to their low mechanical strength, resulting in the loss of enzyme activity [Citation8,Citation19]; (2) since the hybrid nanoflower is essentially a phosphate complex, its solubility in acidic solutions dramatically limits its application [Citation24]; (3) Although the preparation method of nanoflowers is simple, the preparation period is extended, generally taking 3 d to fully form the flower-like structure [Citation63,Citation64]. In response to these problems, a series of proposals have been made to improve the mechanical strength and stability of nanoflowers [Citation92,Citation101].

Supported hybrid nanoflowers

The separation of enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers in reaction systems often requires the help of filtration or centrifugation, and the low mechanical strength of enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers themselves leads to the ineffectiveness of enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers in practical industrial production. In order to improve the disadvantages of low mechanical strength of nanoflowers and not easy to recover, some scholars proposed to prepare supported hybrid nanoflowers by combining nanoflowers with carriers (). Zhu et al. deposited brown algae-Cu3(PO4)2⋅3H2O hybrid nanoflowers on membranes of disposable syringe filters for colorimetric detection of phenol [Citation92]. The membranes containing hybrid nanoflowers can be readily recovered. Zhao et al. prepared α-acetyl lactate decarboxylase (ALDC)-Ca3(PO4)2 hybrid nanoflowers (Ca3(PO4)2-ALDC) and then encapsulated the Ca3(PO4)2-ALDC in alginate gel beads (Ca3(PO4)2-ALDC@Alg). The reusability and stability of Ca3(PO4)2-ALDC@Alg were greatly improved compared to free ALDC and Ca3(PO4)2-ALDC. Similarly, Ren et al. encapsulated lipase hybrid nanoflowers in a composite hydrogel of polyvinyl alcohol and chitosan, which effectively protected the nanoflowers with low mechanical strength from external forces and simplified the separation of the immobilized enzyme from the reaction solution [Citation67]. In addition, enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers were constructed on the surface of nanofibre membranes using copper phosphate as the metal source and BSA, laccase, and HRP, and papain as the protein source [Citation102]. The hybrid nanoflowers synthesized on nanofibrous membranes exhibited enhanced reusability and pH tolerance than free enzymes, indicating the potential application of such composite nanoflowers in biodevices, biosensors, and biocatalysis.

Magnetic hybrid nanoflowers

The loading of magnetic nanoparticles into nanomaterials by embedding and post-synthesis adsorption has become an effective solution to immobilized enzymes on nanomaterials that are difficult to recycle by conventional filtration and centrifugation (). Similarly, the difficult recovery of enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers due to their fragile materials and small size has also been solved with the generation of a large number of magnetic enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers [Citation26]. Ren et al. developed amino-functionalized Fe3O4 nanoparticles to prepare magnetic lipase-Cu3(PO4)2 hybrid nanoflowers, which can be easily recovered from the reaction system by means of an external magnetic field [Citation24]. Zhong et al. prepared magnetic lipase hybrid nanoflows incorporating Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs). It is noteworthy that the magnetic hybrid nanoflower retained its enzymatic activity well in the methanol environment, exhibited good storage stability, and could be easily recovered in the presence of an external magnetic field with minimal damage to the enzymatic activity [Citation103].

Figure 12. Preparation of magnetic enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers.

Other nanoflowers

In recent years, novel synthetic methods have also emerged to improve the stability and synthesis rate of enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers. Liu et al. developed a self-healing nanoflower technology, whereby enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers with sodium alginate (SA) could be self-repaired and reused under acidic conditions [Citation104]. This self-healing property allows the hybridized nanoflowers to maintain high catalytic activity after several uses in low-pH environments. Batule et al. developed an ultra-fast synthesis of enzyme-inorganic nanoflowers through an acoustic-chemical approach [Citation64]. In this case, the enzyme-copper phosphate hybrid nanoflowers could be synthesized at 5 min, which greatly decreases the synthesis time of hybrid nanoflowers. Besides, some studies have shown that the enzymatic properties in enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers can be effectively enhanced by chemical modification of the nanoflower surface [Citation33,Citation105].

Biotechnology applications

In recent years, organic-inorganic hybrid nanoflower and enzyme@MOF composites have been applied extensively in modern biotechnology practice, such as biosensors, biopharmaceuticals, biocatalytic industrial production, wastewater treatment, etc. [Citation3,Citation4] () due to their excellent catalytic efficiency, enhanced stability, as well as commendable specificity and selectivity [Citation8,Citation9,Citation29].

Figure 13. Application of enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers and enzyme-MOF hybrids.

Biosensors

Biosensors and their derived detection devices are becoming more and more important in people’s daily life due to their excellent performances, such as fast, simple, and accurate detection in complex detection environments [Citation106]. In order to face the complexity and diversity of biosensor environments, the enzymes in the sensor systems need to have higher requirements in terms of activity, stability, and sensitivity. In recent years, biosensors based on MOFs@enzyme composites and enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers have attracted more and more attention [Citation107,Citation108].

In terms of MOFs@enzyme, Wang et al. developed a simulated multi-enzyme system to detect glucose concentrations. The formation of the multi-enzyme system was achieved by immobilizing glucose oxidase (GOx) on HP-PCN-224 (Fe) [Citation109]. The resultant GOx@HP-PCN-224(Fe) exhibited excellent sensitivity as a glucose detector with the detection limit being very low (0.87 μM), indicating that this GOx@HP-PCN-224(Fe) had good specificity as a sensitive glucose biosensor. Recently, Feng et al. designed a novel glucose detection system by encapsulating cytochrome C (Cyt C) in the carrier HZIF-8 and then binding GOx to the carrier surface by physical adsorption to form a multi-enzyme system Cyt C@HZIF-8/GOx for the detection of glucose concentration [Citation110] (). The authors confirmed by TEM images the formation of homogeneous spheres by the self-aggregation effect of zinc sulfate (ZnSO4) particles in methanol and observed HZIF-8 with a core-shell structure, multilayered ZIF-8 crystals formed a shell with a thickness of 50 nm. The inner cavity of HZIF-8 contains a block of ZIF-8 nanocrystals and possesses a mesoporous structure with distinct channels. After the immobilization of Cyt C and GOx, no significant morphological changes of HZIF-8 were observed. The results of CLSM showed that Cyt C containing Cyt C labeled by RhB emitted red fluorescence, GOx containing GOx labeled by FITC showed green fluorescence, and HZIF-8 with Cyt C and GOx produced two types of fluorescence, where the green fluorescence was on the surface and shell layer of the carrier, while the red fluorescence was on the interior. This result confirms that RhB-Cyt C and FITC-GOx are present in both Cyt C@HZIF-8/GOx. Furthermore, the adsorption/desorption isotherms of N2 show that HZIF-8 displays a type IV isotherm with a clear hysteresis loop at high relative pressure, indicating the presence of mesopores. The generation of mesopores on its surface was attributed by the authors to the interstices between the small ZIF-8 crystals that form the walls of the spherical shell. The hierarchical microporous and mesoporous ZIF-8 with core-shell superstructure can be used as a suitable carrier for enzyme immobilization. The immobilized enzyme system displayed excellent linearity at low glucose concentrations, a low limit of detection (0.8 μM), and a rapid reaction (reaction time of approximately 10 min). The presence of the mesoporous structure of HZIF-8 is more conducive to the diffusion of the intermediate product transport, and the results obtained by kinetic analysis of the enzymatic reaction exhibit lower Km values for Cyt C@HZIF-8/GOx than for conventional Cyt C@ZIF-8/Gox [Citation111]. Sun et al. developed a copper phosphate hybrid nanoflower based on a dual enzyme system of glucose oxidase(GOx) as well as HPR as a colorimetric glucose sensor [Citation65]. Zhu et al. prepared GOx-HPR-Cu3(PO4)2 hybrid nanoflowers on a proposed microfluidic paper-based analytical device. The biometric biosensor exhibited rapid and accurate concentration quantification of glucose concentration, providing technical support for the detection of human blood glucose levels [Citation112].

Figure 14. Schematic for synthesis process of Cyt C@HZIF-8 and Cyt C@HZIF-8/GOx. Reproduced from ref. [Citation90] with permission from Elsevier, Copyright 2022.

In terms of enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers, Sun et al. developed a hybrid calcium phosphate nanoflower (PAL@NF) based on phenylalanine aminolyase (PAL) and calcium phosphate. The resultant PAL hybrid nanoflowers retain more than 90% of the enzymatic activity compared to the free enzyme [Citation11]. Furthermore, the PAL@NF displayed highly resistant to adverse external factors as well as repeatable. In addition, the paper-based biosensor with the PAL@NF was prepared and exhibited a wide detection range and fast response, providing a valuable reference for its wide range of practical applications.

In addition, MOFs@enzyme composites and enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers have also exhibited good results in the detection of various compounds, such as hydrogen peroxide [Citation113], vitamin C [Citation114], phenolic substances [Citation115], sodium urate [Citation116], prothrombin [Citation117], BPA/E [Citation118,Citation119], as well as parathion methyl [Citation120,Citation121].

Biomedicine

In recent years, immobilized enzymes and their derivatives have shown great potential for diagnostic and therapeutic applications in diseases, and relevant research results have been reported in anti-cancer and bacterial inhibition [Citation122]. Several immobilized enzymes based on MOFs are well exploited for cancer therapy due to their unique pH-responsive release properties, making them excellent drug delivery vehicles [Citation122]. Duan et al. developed an efficient and concise antigen delivery system for cancer immunotherapy by encapsulating tumour-associated antigenic macromolecules in MOFs [Citation123]. Zhang et al. prepared a hybrid combining ZIF-8 with the anti-cancer drug doxorubicin (DOX). The resultant ZIF-8@DOX exhibited highly effective and stable for cancer treatment [Citation124]. Recently, a novel MOF composite, CpG@OVA@ZIF-8, has been used in the development and use of vaccines. The CpG@OVA@ZIF-8 could enhance the systemic immune response and induce an effective immune memory response [Citation125]. Furthermore, DOX was loaded in graphene quantum dots (GQD)@ZIF-8 nanoparticles. The resultant MOF composites exhibited a good synergistic effect with higher clearance of cancer cells [Citation126]. Besides, a novel NPCN-333 complex with immobilizing tyrosinase (TYR) was developed and exhibited promising results for cancer treatment [Citation127]. The TYR@NPCN-333 was able to continuously activate paracetamol prodrug (APAP) in cancer cells. This novel MOF@enzyme reactor could be used in the biomedical industry for different practical applications. Furthermore, Farha et al. developed a strategy to encapsulate organophosphate anhydrase (OPAA, a nerve agent detoxifying enzyme) in PCN-128y. The resultant OPAA@PCN-128y showed significant detoxification effects [Citation128] ().

Figure 15. Organophosphate anhydrase (OPAA) successfully encapsulated in a water-stable zirconium metal-organic backbone (MOF). Adapted with permission from ref. [Citation109]. Copyright 2016 American Chemical Society.

Cancer cell growth requires an exogenous environment to provide oxygen and glucose, and when these exogenous conditions are blocked, the effect of inhibiting cancer cell growth and multiplication is achieved. Based on this principle, a MOF nano-bioreactor with tetrazolamide (TPZ) and GOx was developed. Then, this bioreactor (TGZ@eM) was encapsulated by the membrane of red blood cells. The acidic environment induced by intracellular lysosomes breaks down TGZ@eM to deliver GOx and TPZ. The released GOx consumes exogenous glucose and oxygen to inhibit the growth of cancer cells [Citation129]. To build a system for the detection and treatment of macular disease, Chen et al. co-immobilized GOx, insulin, and anti-VEGF inducer in ZIF-8 [Citation130]. In the system, GOx-catalyzed oxidation of glucose can dynamically modulate the controlled release of VEGF inducers as a means to inhibit VEGF-induced angiogenesis and reduce glucose concentrations in the growth environment of cancer cells, providing a reference for their clinical treatment.

In contrast to MOFs@enzyme composites, enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers have been mostly used for bacteriostatic purposes. Baldemir et al. prepared hybrid nanoflowers using green tea extracts, which consist mainly of catechins and caffeine [Citation131]. The hybrid nanoflower showed excellent inhibition of the fungus. The hydrogen peroxide produced during the oxidation of glucose exhibits good broad-spectrum antibacterial effects against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative microorganisms. Based on this phenomenon, GOx-based hybrid nanoflowers have been reported to achieve bacterial inhibition through their oxidative decomposition of glucose [Citation132]. Another hybrid nanoflower was synthesized using polydopamine with in situ reduction of AgNPs in it [Citation133]. There is potential for a wide range of clinical applications as the resulting hybrid nanoflowers exhibit better antibacterial activity and maintain low cytotoxicity to mammalian cells.

Biocatalysis

As immobilized enzymes, enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers and enzyme@MOF composites were widely used to produce and prepare high-value-added destination products, such as: chemical materials, pharmaceutical intermediates, food additives, cosmetics, etc. The main reactions involved in biocatalysis are oxidation-reduction reactions, hydrolysis reactions, Michael addition reactions, esterification reactions, and transesterification reactions [Citation25,Citation29].

In terms of enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers, to catalyze the epoxidation reaction of vegetable oils, Hua et al. prepared lipase@Cu3(PO4)2 hybrid nanoflowers by combining lipase with copper phosphate [Citation134]. Surprisingly, the lipase blend of nanoflowers exhibited extremely promising catalytic activity. Yu et al. found that papain@Cu3(PO4)2 nanoflowers catalyzed a higher yield than free papain in a noevenagel condensation reaction using benzaldehyde and acetylacetone as substrates [Citation135]. Besides, Thermomyces lanuginosus lipase (TLL)@Ca3(PO4)2-Bone hybrid nanoflowers exhibited the advantage of being reusable several times and stable in terms of enzymatic activity at high temperatures [Citation136]. Zhong et al. prepared magnetic lipase hybrid nanoflowers. Derived from Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) embedded in the hybrid nanoflower, the magnetic lipase hybrid nanoflower could be rapidly recycled by external magnetic forces. Furthermore, the magnetic lipase hybrid nanoflowers showed better tolerance and storage stability under methanol compared to free lipase [Citation103]. In the reaction for biodiesel production from soybean oil, biodiesel yield by the magnetic lipase hybrid nanoflowers reached 88%, whereas free lipase was only 69%. Furthermore, the biodiesel yield by the magnetic lipase hybrid nanoflowers still remained at 76% after 6 consecutive cycles. In addition, Patel et al. developed hybrid nanoflowers based on recombinant l-arabinol 4-dehydrogenase for the enhanced production of the rare sugar l-xylulose [Citation137]. The hybrid nanoflower exhibited excellent enzymatic properties. To resolve (R,S)-2-pentyl alcohol in organic solvents, Wu et al. synthesized laccase-Cu3(PO4)2 hybrid nanoflowers using vinyl acetate as an acyl donor [Citation69]. The resulting hybrid nanoflower was able to maintain the same enzymatic activity and enantioselectivity as the initial reaction after multiple uses.

Besides, enzyme-MOFs composites have already been used in the biocatalysis field. For example, Xie et al. prepared cellulase@UiO-66-NH2 by immobilizing cellulase on UIO-66-NH2 by physical adsorption for catalyzing the hydrolysis of insoluble cellulose to produce soluble reducing sugars [Citation138]. The cellulase@UiO-66-NH2 exhibited better thermal stability, pH tolerance, and reusability than the free enzyme. Hu et al. found that immobilized lipase on UIO-66 with hydrophobic treatment exhibited higher activity than immobilized lipase on UIO-66 without hydrophobic treatment [Citation139]. The immobilized lipase on UIO-66 exhibited excellent reusability. Besides, Zhong et al. used a vapor deposition (CVD) method to uniformly coat ZIF-L with hydrophobic polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) and applied it to immobilize lipase from Aspergillus niger (AOL) for the conversion of biodiesel [Citation140] (). The results showed that the PDMS coating enhanced the stability of ZIF-8 and ZIF-L in PBS. The immobilized lipase (AOL@PDMS-ZIF-L) exhibited better enzyme protein loading and activity recovery than AOL@ZIF-L.

Figure 16. Schematic illustration of the preparation process for the AOL@PDMS-ZIF-L and AOL@ZIF-L. Reproduced from ref. [Citation121] with permission from Elsevier, Copyright 2021.

Wastewater treatment

Nowadays, the effective treatment of harmful compounds in wastewater is particularly important due to the scarcity of water resources and the unpredictable situation of pollution [Citation141,Citation142].

Being representative enzyme families for the degradation of wastewater, such as dyes and other undesirable compounds, enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflower and enzyme@MOF composites based on laccase and peroxidase enzymes have also been widely studied for wastewater treatment [Citation143].

A unique cross-linked protein-metal hybrid nanoflower system (CL-NF) with an encapsulation rate of nearly 80% has been proposed by cross-linking the prepared laccase-hybrid nanoflowers using glutaraldehyde as a cross-linking agent and displayed a doubling of its enzymatic activity compared to the free laccase [Citation22]. To address the problem of high levels of bisphenol A (BPA) in sewage in water pollution, Fu et al. developed a magnetic hybrid laccase-based nanoflower and used it to degrade BPA in water. In this case, the introduction of magnetic nanoparticles offers the possibility for the hybrid nanoflower to be magnetically recycled, reducing the industrial cost of practical use [Citation144].

Meanwhile, Wu et al. successfully prepared an amino-functionalized magnetic metal-organic framework (MOF) Fe3O4-NH2@MIL-101(Cr) for the immobilization of laccase [Citation145]. Fe3O4-NH2@MIL-101(Cr)@laccase exhibited good enzyme activity recovery, environmental tolerance, and reusability. The immobilized laccase was used to remove 2,4-dichlorophenol. The removal efficiency reached 87%. Alhayali and coworkers prepared peroxidase hybrid nanoflowers with Fe3O4 nanoparticles (peroxidase/Fe3O4@Cu2+ HNFs). The peroxidase/Fe3O4@Cu2+ HNFs exhibited excellent catalytic reduction for nitroaniline and azo dyes [Citation146].

Food industry

For the food industry, the practical application of immobilized enzymes can be found in food processing, food safety testing, and food packaging [Citation147–149]. Similarly, enzyme@MOFs and hybrid nanoflowers have been widely used in the food industry such as food analysis and food packaging [Citation150].

Atiroğlu et al. immobilized α-amylase in boswellia-bovine serum albumin@zeolite imidazole ester backbone nanocomposites (OLB/BSA@ZIF-8-α-amylase) [Citation151]. The resultant OLB/BSA@ZIF-8-α-amylase exhibited significantly enhanced pH and thermal stability than the free enzyme. Meanwhile, the OLB/BSA@ZIF-8-α-amylase decreased only less than 10% of its initial activity after 8 weeks of storage, indicating excellent storage stability. Furthermore, the OLB/BSA@ZIF-8-α-amylase retained over 81% of its initial activity after 20 cycles, demonstrating excellent reusability. In order to achieve efficient conversion of cellulose to glucose, cellulose hydrolase (CBH), endoglucanase (EG), and β-glucosidase (BG) were co-immobilized on a binary tag consisting of the elastin-like polypeptide (ELP) and His tag to prepare a multi-enzyme hybrid nanoflower (ECG-NF) [Citation152]. The highest alginate yield reached 67.7%. Meanwhile, they found that the ZIF-8 coating provided a protection for enzymes. To solve the allergic problems, Feng et al. synthesized a novel magnetic papain-Cu3(PO4)2 hybrid nanoflowers (PCMNs). The PCMNs could hydrolyze most of the allergenic proteins in cow’s milk to produce hypersensitive milk [Citation153]. Furthermore, the PCMNs also showed good reusability in cyclic analysis and were easily separated from the product.

In the context of food detection, acetylcholinesterase, horseradish peroxidase, and choline oxidase were encapsulated in a cruciferous zeolite imidazolium ester backbone 8 (CF-ZIF-8) by one-step co-precipitation to construct a multi-enzyme platform for sensitive glucose detection [Citation154]. The multi-enzyme platform showed excellent performance and a low detection limit for acetamiprid. Hu et al. developed a new hybrid nanoenzyme/natural enzyme hybrid biosensor (MIL-88B(Fe)-NH2@GLOX). The MIL-88B(Fe)-NH2@GLOX exhibited a wide linear range (1-100 μM) with a low limit of glutamate detection of 2.5 μM [Citation155].

Summary and outlook

In this paper, we summarized the common synthesis methods of enzyme@MOF composites, and enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers, as well as novel improved synthesizing strategies and potential biotechnological applications. On the one hand, to address the problems of poor immobilization efficiency and catalyst stability of the prepared enzyme@MOF composites by common methods, aperture engineering was proposed to improve the internal environment of the pores of MOFs or to expand the pores to obtain higher immobilization efficiency and applicability of enzyme species. MOF post-modification immobilization methods were suggested changing the contact mode between MOF and enzyme moleculesto to obtain more stable catalysts. In addition, the ligand hybrid functionalization strategy can bring new physical and chemical properties to the enzyme@MOF composites. On the other hand, to address the problems of difficult recovery and poor tolerance of enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers, combining enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers with a carriers strategy was to proposed to improve stability and facilitate the sustainability of catalytic reactions. In particular, magnetic enzyme-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers with magnetic nanoparticles could be easily isolated from the reaction system under the action of an external magnetic field without centrifugation and filtration. Meanwhile, the authors summarize the practical cases of organic-inorganic hybrid nanoflowers and enzyme@MOF composites applied to modern biotechnology practice in recent years, such as biosensors, biopharmaceuticals, biocatalytic industrial production, wastewater treatment, etc., which provide references for their practical industrial applications and development. Besides, it is highly valuable and promising research to explore and develop new methods for the construction of ligand hybridization functionalisation strategies. In the authors’ opinion, the following aspects can be attempted: (1) new enzyme@MOF composites hybrid materials need to be developed; (2) the interaction relationship between MOF hybrid materials and enzyme molecules, especially the law of material influence on the structure of immobilized enzyme molecules, needs to be further investigated. In conclusion, it is believed that through the efforts of researchers, hybrid MOF materials with excellent properties can be used more and more widely in the field of biocatalysis.

Acknowledgements

This work is financially by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (project No. 22108206, 21676069); Project of Guangxi Key Research and Development program, China (project no. GuikeAB21238005); Postgraduate Research Innovation project of Tianjin (project No. 2020YJSB122).

Disclosure statement

No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s).

Additional information

Funding

The author(s) reported there is no funding associated with the work featured in this article.

                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        Donation(捐赠)
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        EasyPubMedicine is completely free. If you find it useful, will you consider supporting us a cup of coffee($5)?
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        EasyPubMedicine是完全免费的。如果您觉得此扩展有用、好用，能否请作者吃一份烤冷面（10元），喝一杯咖啡（20元）呢？

                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        Tian Zhen: A medical student majored in dermatology with great interest in programming.
